enrichment.summary: Kyle gives a sprawling, high-level history of the papacy from Jesus and the apostles through the papal states era, the Reformation, and the current pope, covering corruption, the Crusades, the Medici influence, and Martin Luther's break with Rome. He names his top three best popes (Peter, Gregory the Great, the current Pope Francis) and worst popes (Urban II, John XII, Gregory XI), and closes with thoughts on the Catholic Church's vast wealth and what he thinks the current pope should do about it. Throughout, he frames historical events through an analogy of the papacy as a person shaped by its environment.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Peter (first pope), Gregory the Great, and the current Pope Francis for returning to Christian basics
Urban II for starting the Crusades, John XII for turning the papacy into a brothel, Gregory XI for the Great Schism
The Catholic Church should digitize the Vatican library, sell or donate church buildings as homeless shelters, and divest its wealth to actually follow Jesus's teachings
Likes him for humility and focus on the poor, but thinks he should go further and give up the Church's wealth
Bad because it involved killing Muslims who also believed in God, though they had the side effect of bringing Middle Eastern culture to Western Europe
The worst era for the papacy — mixing political power with spiritual authority corrupts both
Believes Jesus was an alien with superpowers who spread a genuinely superior moral religion
Pure equal pay communism is crazy, but extreme wealth inequality should be kept in check — closer to social democratic than Marxist
The printing press letting people read the Bible themselves exposed how far the Church had strayed; the Reformation was a correction
Actually helped bring the Catholic Church back toward its roots and made it powerful again

claims.subject: the Vatican
claims.claim: The Vatican is the smallest country in the world.

claims.subject: the papacy
claims.claim: The papacy is the oldest continual line of leadership in the world, going back 2000 years.

claims.subject: the papacy
claims.claim: The papacy is the only ancient political structure that is an elected autocracy.

claims.subject: history
claims.claim: History should be looked at as though it’s a living person to understand cause and effect.

claims.subject: the papacy
claims.claim: The papacy has changed over time, with some popes being greedy and others truly religious.

claims.subject: Jesus
claims.claim: Jesus started Christianity by preaching the New Testament against the old traditions of the Jews.

claims.subject: Jesus
claims.claim: Jesus performed miracles because he was an alien with superpowers.

claims.subject: Christianity
claims.claim: Christianity caught on because it was a superior religion that didn’t require trinkets or temple fees.

claims.subject: Jesus
claims.claim: Jesus was born in 7 BC and died in 33 AD, around the age of 40.

claims.subject: the New Testament
claims.claim: The New Testament started being written down about 30 years after Jesus’s death.

claims.subject: apostle Peter
claims.claim: Apostle Peter was the first pope, appointed by Jesus.

claims.subject: the Holy Spirit
claims.claim: The Holy Spirit refers to alien technology that gave the apostles superpowers.

claims.subject: Christianity
claims.claim: Christianity spread well in Jewish, Greek, and pagan communities.

claims.subject: Rome
claims.claim: Rome was a multicultural city where Christianity caught on well.

claims.subject: Constantine
claims.claim: Constantine made Christianity the official religion of the Roman Empire in 325 AD.

claims.subject: the Bible
claims.claim: Constantine commissioned the assembly of the Old and New Testament books.

claims.subject: the papacy
claims.claim: The papacy became a political entity during the Papal States period.

claims.subject: Gregory the Great
claims.claim: Gregory the Great was one of the greatest popes, focusing on helping the poor.

claims.subject: the Catholic Church
claims.claim: The Catholic Church went through fluctuations of wealth and poverty, similar to a person’s life.

claims.subject: the Papal States
claims.claim: The Papal States were a country owned by the papacy from 752 to 1870.

claims.subject: Charlemagne
claims.claim: Charlemagne was crowned emperor of Rome by the pope in 800 AD.

claims.subject: the Crusades
claims.claim: The Crusades brought Middle Eastern culture and education to Western Europe.

claims.subject: Urban II
claims.claim: Urban II was the worst pope because he started the First Crusade.

claims.subject: the Medici family
claims.claim: The Medici family used their wealth to influence the papacy during the Renaissance.

claims.subject: the Sistine Chapel
claims.claim: The Sistine Chapel was painted during the Renaissance, funded by selling indulgences.

claims.subject: Martin Luther
claims.claim: Martin Luther’s 95 Theses criticized the Catholic Church for selling indulgences.

claims.subject: the Jesuits
claims.claim: The Jesuits traveled the world to spread Christianity and help natives.

claims.subject: the current pope
claims.claim: The current pope is focusing on helping the poor and being humble.

claims.subject: the Vatican
claims.claim: The Vatican should digitize its library and turn churches into homeless shelters.
